ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for curry puff; A0CKP (filled pastry) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for curry puff as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard curry puff weighing approximately 65 g, as typically served at Singaporean coffee shops and bakeries such as Toast Box.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Puff (Karipap) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for fried filled pastry
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists curry puff (karipap) with potato-curry filling. Values per 100 g are adapted from HPB Foodo entry for curry puff and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried filled pastry (NDB 18302) to confirm macro plausibility. The Toast Box variant is a standard fried/baked curry puff with potato-curry filling; no branded nutrient panel is publicly available, so HPB Foodo values are used as the primary reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of savoury pastry / filled pastry analogous estimate based on potato-filled pastry data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Singaporean curry puff is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated from the closest analogous food: potato-filled savoury pastry / samosa-type pastry, which the University of Sydney GI Database reports in the range 50–58 (low to medium). Given 54 is <= 55, it is classified as low GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 21.8) / 100 ≈ 11.8, where carbohydrate per 65 g serving = 33.5 × 0.65 = 21.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~247 mg per 65 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Each curry puff contributes approximately 247 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit intake to one puff per occasion and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as soy sauce-based dishes or processed snacks.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~54) but moderate carbohydrate load (~21.8 g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: While the glycemic index is low (54), the glycemic load of approximately 11.8 per puff can still cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; people with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict consumption to one puff and pair with a low-GI food or protein source to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.4 g per serving) and trans fat (~0.1 g per serving) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried pastry shell contributes saturated and trans fats that may adversely affect LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit curry puff consumption to occasional treats and prefer baked variants where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A curry puff (Malay: karipap; Chinese: 咖喱角) is a popular Singaporean and Malaysian savoury snack consisting of a flaky or shortcrust wheat-flour pastry shell encasing a spiced filling, most commonly curried potato with or without chicken, egg, or sardine. The pastry is typically deep-fried until golden and crisp, though baked versions are also available. The filling is warmly spiced with curry powder, turmeric, and onion, producing a fragrant, mildly pungent flavour with a soft, starchy interior contrasting the crisp exterior. Curry puffs are a staple of Singaporean coffee shops (kopitiams), hawker centres, and bakery chains such as Toast Box, where they are sold individually or in promotional bundles. Nutritionally, a standard 65 g curry puff provides approximately 194 kcal, 10.5 g fat, 21.8 g carbohydrate, and 3.8 g protein. The low glycemic index (~54) but moderate glycemic load (~11.8 per puff) and moderate sodium content (~247 mg per puff) make them a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation. The dish reflects the multicultural culinary heritage of Singapore, drawing on South Asian curry traditions adapted into a portable pastry format widely adopted across Malay, Chinese, and Indian communities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a curry puff?
How much sodium in a curry puff?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo curry puff entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central fried filled pastry data. The Toast Box variant does not have a publicly available branded nutrient panel. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using potato-filled savoury pastry as the closest match. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact branded match was available.
notes: 1. The input food_name 'Buy 5 Curry Puffs and get 1 FREE' is a promotional bundle description from field survey data (Toast Box - Funan). The canonical food name has been normalised to 'Curry Puff' for database purposes. 2. The cuisine tag in the input was 'Toast' (referring to the Toast Box restaurant chain), which has been corrected to 'Singaporean' as the appropriate cuisine classification. 3. Nutrient values are per 100 g of curry puff (fried, potato-curry filling) based on HPB Foodo data. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food item. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food item. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this food item. 7. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using potato-filled savoury pastry as the closest defensible match; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean curry puff was found in the database. 8. Halal status is marked true as Toast Box is a halal-certified chain in Singapore; kosher status is false as no certification is claimed. 9. The promotional bundle price of SGD 10.00 for 6 puffs (buy 5 get 1 free) implies approximately SGD 1.67 per puff.
